main_summary.summary: Walter Tillow, 73 at the time of this 2013 interview in Louisville, traces his path from a Bronx childhood shaped by his parents' Young Communist League background and the 1953 Rosenberg execution, through undergraduate organizing at Harpur College, to full-time Civil Rights Movement work. After attending SNCC's Easter 1963 conference at Gammon Theological Seminary in Atlanta, where he met Anne Braden, Tillow went to Fayette County, Tennessee in summer 1963 to do voter registration with John McFerren and Marcheka McCracken's community church network. Following the March on Washington, he joined SNCC's Atlanta communications office under Julian Bond, producing press releases, the Student Voice newsletter, and the unreleased Freedom in the Air SNCC record. In spring 1964, Ella Baker and Bob Moses assigned him to open SNCC's Washington office for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge to the Atlantic City Democratic National Convention, where he worked alongside Frank Smith and Barbara Jones to organize credentials-committee strategy, lobby state delegations, and assemble the minority-report and roll-call-vote machinery. He details the compromise pressure exerted by Hubert Humphrey, Walter Reuther, and Joseph Rauh, and recounts the post-MFDP turn to labor organizing with UE, 1199 SEIU, the Southern Student Organizing Committee, and the Communist Party.
key_themes: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party 1964 Atlantic City challenge
SNCC Atlanta communications office and press operations
main_summary.historical_significance:
Tillow provides a detailed inside-the-machinery account of the MFDP credentials-committee strategy at the 1964 Democratic National Convention, including the precise procedural mechanics (10% threshold for a minority report, the 10-delegation requirement for a roll-call vote), the named arm-twisting of credentials-committee delegates by the Johnson administration through Hubert Humphrey and Walter Reuther, and the specific delegates who held firm (Robert Kastenmeier, Edith Green) versus those who were forced to recant (Joseph Rauh, an unnamed California woman whose husband faced a judicial-appointment threat). His testimony names Reggie Robinson's role in securing the Atlantic City hotel for the 68-delegate MFDP contingent and identifies the closed-door meeting with Bayard Rustin, Ella Baker, and Frank Smith that preceded the convention.

The interview also documents the practical infrastructure of SNCC's 1963-64 Atlanta operation: the 3.5-and-Hunter Street office layout, the IBM Selectric and A.B. Dick offset-printing transition, the WATS-line campaign that forced the Bonanza cast to cancel a segregated Jackson Mississippi appearance, and the pressing-but-never-distributed Freedom in the Air SNCC record. Tillow's longitudinal arc from SNCC through UE, 1199 SEIU, the Southern Student Organizing Committee co-founding with Sue Thrasher and Sam Shirah, and the 1968 King-Spock and Dick Gregory-Mark Lane Peace and Freedom Party ballot drives, establishes how the labor-left and civil rights movements remained organizationally entangled through the late 1960s and 1970s.

chapters.title: Bronx Childhood and Cold War Family
chapters.summary: Tillow describes being born in 1940 in the Bronx to parents who had been Young Communist League members in the 1920s. He recounts the 1953 Rosenberg execution (he was 13), his father taking him to the funeral, and the wider Cold War atmosphere of a half-radical, half-conservative extended family, including aunts who were Communist Party members or sympathizers.

chapters.title: Race in 1950s New York
chapters.summary: Tillow recalls Parkchester, the Metropolitan Life-funded apartment development outside his Bronx neighborhood that excluded African Americans by holding apartments vacant for a year to escape rent control. He describes the chain-yourself-to-the-door housing protests of the period as the same movement that produced the Anne and Carl Braden housing work in Louisville.

chapters.title: Harpur College Activism
chapters.summary: As a junior at Harpur College in Endicott, New York in 1960, Tillow joined classmates to picket the local Woolworth in sympathy with the Southern sit-in movement. He describes defying the New York State civil-defense take-cover drill on the new campus, founding a Young Socialists chapter, and the campus fight to invite Herbert Aptheker and the Socialist Workers Party candidate to speak, which broke the college president's power to bar speakers.

chapters.title: Cornell and SNCC's Easter Conference
chapters.summary: Tillow describes graduate work at the University of Washington (1961-62) and Cornell (1962-63), where during the New York newspaper strike he read the Baltimore Afro-American for a campus clipping circle. The Afro's coverage of SNCC's Easter 1963 conference at Gammon Theological Seminary in Atlanta drew a carload of Cornell students south, where Tillow met Anne Braden.

chapters.title: Fayette County Tennessee Voter Registration
chapters.summary: Tillow, Charlie and Bunny Haney with their infant spent summer 1963 in Fayette County, Tennessee, working with John McFerren and Marcheka McCracken's community church on voter registration. He describes the Tent City origins of the local movement (Korean War vets registering after a 1959 jury-selection trial), the slow registration procedures at the courthouse, a self-help leather book-bag project in Haywood County, and being jumped on the road by a lone white assailant.

chapters.title: SNCC Atlanta Communications Office
chapters.summary: After the March on Washington, Tillow joined SNCC's Atlanta headquarters working under Julian Bond in the communications office at 3.5 and Hunter Street, alongside Bob Wald, Mike Sayer, Dinky Romilly, James Forman, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson. He details producing tri-weekly press releases, the Student Voice newsletter mailed to thousands of Northern friends-of-SNCC, the IBM Selectric typewriter transition, and the A.B. Dick offset-press operation set up by Mark Schoenfeld next door.

chapters.title: WATS Line Campaigns and Freedom in the Air
chapters.summary: Tillow recounts SNCC's WATS-line operation tracking traveling entertainers to pressure them out of segregated Jackson Mississippi appearances, successfully getting the Bonanza cast to cancel. He describes the SNCC press release on Byron De La Beckwith's federal land sale during the Medgar Evers trial, and the pressed-but-undistributed Freedom in the Air SNCC record featuring Bernice Johnson Reagon, Cordell Reagon, Bertha Gober, Ruth-Etta Harris, and Charles Sherrod.
keywords: Freedom in the Air SNCC record

chapters.title: Opening the MFDP Washington Office
chapters.summary: In spring 1964, Ella Baker and Bob Moses assigned Tillow to open SNCC's Washington DC office at 14th and U Street to coordinate the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge to the Democratic National Convention. He worked with Frank Smith from Mississippi and Barbara Jones, traveled to Minnesota, Wisconsin, and Michigan state Democratic conventions seeking resolutions, and met with Bayard Rustin, Jim Forman, and Ella Baker in Rustin's New York office on strategy.

chapters.title: Credentials Committee Strategy
chapters.summary: Tillow explains the procedural mechanics: a minority report required 10% of credentials-committee delegates, and a roll-call vote on either majority or minority report required 10 delegations to support it. He names Wayne Morse's swap (resolutions over credentials for Edith Green of Oregon), Robert Kastenmeier of Wisconsin as a strong supporter, the Mississippi white delegation that sent a no-name slate to avoid drawing attention, and the parallel work of pressuring the parents of Mississippi Freedom Summer volunteers.

chapters.title: Atlantic City Convention
chapters.summary: Tillow describes Reggie Robinson securing a 100-room Atlantic City hotel for the 68-delegate MFDP contingent co-chaired by Aaron Henry (NAACP Mississippi president) and Ed King (Tougaloo theology professor), with Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray as principal women. He details Hamer's televised credentials-committee testimony that moved Edith Green and prompted Johnson to call an emergency press conference to cut away from coverage, the 24-hour boardwalk vigil where delegates surrendered badges, and the Mississippi white delegation walking out after the hearing.

chapters.title: The Compromise and Disillusionment
chapters.summary: Tillow names the arm-twisting that produced the two-observer-seats compromise: Walter Reuther threatened Joseph Rauh's UAW general counsel income, an unnamed African American woman lost her husband's California judgeship, and only Robert Kastenmeier and Edith Green held the credentials-committee line. He recounts the church meeting where Bayard Rustin, Martin Luther King Jr, James Farmer, Jack Pratt of the National Council of Churches, and Wayne Morse pressed the MFDP delegates to accept the compromise, which the delegates refused. Rowland Evans and Robert Novak's syndicated red-baiting column followed.

chapters.title: Congressional Challenge and Aftermath
chapters.summary: Tillow describes the 1965 MFDP challenge to the seating of three Mississippi Delta congressmen including Jamie Whitten, led by William Kunstler, Arthur Kinoy, John Conyers, and Elizabeth Holtzman, in which Tip O'Neill forced the three to stand aside on the opening day of the new House session. He observes that movement attention turned to Alabama and Stokely Carmichael's Lowndes County work, and that the 1968 Chicago convention's seating of Julian Bond's Georgia delegation flowed from the 1964 rules fight.

chapters.title: Southern Student Organizing Committee and Labor Bridge
chapters.summary: Working with Carl Braden and Jim Williams, Tillow helped found the Southern Student Organizing Committee through a Nashville meeting with Sue Thrasher and Sam Shirah. He organized Highlander Research and Education Center workshops linking SNCC organizers with Jesse Prosten of the Amalgamated Meat Cutters, Bob Lewis of UE, an Atlanta Teamsters business agent, and ILGWU staff, attempting to bring labor money and influence into Southern civil-rights organizing.

chapters.title: UE, Lynchburg, and Marriage to Kay Marlowe
chapters.summary: Tillow met Kay Marlowe at a Highlander workshop while she was working in eastern Kentucky for the Berman Gibson-led Wildcat-strike Committee for Miners coalition organized by Paul O'Dwyer and Stanley Aronowitz. Both joined UE: Kay went to Honetown, Pennsylvania to organize Slovenian workers; Tillow went to Lynchburg, Virginia to try to organize a General Electric plant, where he observed the William Kunstler-Phillip Hirschkop defense of a Black youth in a rape case. They married and moved to UE's Pittsburgh district office.
related_events: Eastern Kentucky wildcat strike Committee for Miners

chapters.title: Pittsburgh Anti-War, 1199 SEIU, and Communist Party
chapters.summary: The Tillows worked through the 1965 spring Washington peace demonstrations and Pittsburgh anti-Vietnam War organizing. Tillow helped circulate petitions for a King-Spock 1968 presidential ticket, then placed Dick Gregory and Mark Lane on the Pennsylvania ballot via the Peace and Freedom Party. He joined 1199 SEIU's Pennsylvania hospital-worker organizing under the 1969 Public Employee Relations Act, then joined the Communist Party as unpaid staff after the 1972 McGovern campaign. Tillow worked on Conyers's 1978 short-work-week bill and Chile solidarity organizing around the Salvador Allende coup.

chapters.title: Camden Print Shop and Move to Louisville
chapters.summary: From 1990 to 1995, Tillow ran the Camden, New Jersey print shop that produced the People's Weekly World. Kay's nursing-organizing work for an NEA affiliate in Paducah and Louisville (Lourdes Hospital and Audubon Hospital) drew the couple to Kentucky, where they settled near Kay's mother in Metropolis, Illinois.

chapters.title: Reflections on Democratic Party Illusions
chapters.summary: Tillow closes by arguing the 1964 MFDP fight stripped away movement illusions about the Democratic Party. He credits the Eisenhower-appointed Fifth Circuit judges (Minor Wisdom, Elbert Tuttle, Frank Johnson Jr) as the judicial backbone that saved civil-rights litigation, contrasting them with Kennedy's Eastland-and-Stennis-approved appointees. He names local movement lawyers Ben Smith and Bruce Waltzer (New Orleans), Len Holt, Ernest Goodman and George Crockett, William Kunstler, and Arthur Kinoy as the legal infrastructure of Movement-era civil rights work.

loc_video.caption: Walter Tillow oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Louisville, Kentucky, 2013 June 21
